name: Active Scanning
description: Adversaries may execute active reconnaissance scans to gather information that can be used during targeting, such as scanning IP blocks and ports.
detection: Monitor for suspicious network traffic that could be indicative of scanning, such as large quantities of requests from a single source.
mitigations: Pre-compromise detection is difficult; monitor external-facing assets

name: Scanning IP Blocks
description: Adversaries may scan victim IP blocks to gather information for targeting, identifying active hosts and services.
detection: Monitor for suspicious inbound network traffic from unexpected sources scanning multiple ports or hosts.

name: Gather Victim Identity Information
description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's identity that can be used during targeting, such as employee names, email addresses, and credentials.
detection: Monitor for suspicious access to social media, corporate directories, or data breach databases.
mitigations: Limit public exposure of employee information

name: Gather Victim Network Information
description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's networks that can be used during targeting, including network topology, DNS records, and IP ranges.
detection: Monitor for excessive DNS queries or WHOIS lookups from suspicious sources.
mitigations: Use privacy protection on domain registrations

name: Phishing for Information
description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to elicit sensitive information that can be used during targeting, without delivering malware.
detection: Monitor email for suspicious messages requesting sensitive information from unknown or spoofed senders.

name: Acquire Infrastructure
description: Adversaries may buy, lease, or rent infrastructure such as servers, domains, and third-party web services for use during targeting.
detection: Monitor for newly registered domains or infrastructure that correlates with known threat actor patterns.

name: Obtain Capabilities
description: Adversaries may buy, steal, or download software tools, exploits, and certificates that can be used during targeting.
detection: Monitor dark web forums and malware repositories for tools associated with threat actors targeting your organization.

name: Compromise Infrastructure
description: Adversaries may compromise third-party infrastructure to use in support of targeting, such as web servers for watering hole attacks.
detection: Monitor for changes in third-party infrastructure that may indicate compromise, such as unexpected DNS changes.

name: Develop Capabilities
description: Adversaries may build custom capabilities such as malware, exploits, and tools for use in operations.
detection: Difficult to detect pre-compromise; monitor for novel malware samples matching known actor TTPs.

name: Phishing
description: Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems. All forms of phishing are electronically delivered social engineering.
detection: Monitor email gateways for suspicious attachments and links. Analyze email headers for spoofing indicators.

name: Spearphishing Attachment
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.
detection: Monitor for suspicious email attachments, especially executables, scripts, and macro-enabled documents.

name: Spearphishing Link
description: Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious link in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.
detection: Monitor for suspicious URLs in emails and web proxy logs for connections to known malicious domains.

name: Exploit Public-Facing Application
description: Adversaries may attempt to take advantage of a weakness in an Internet-facing computer or program using software, data, or commands to cause unintended behavior.
detection: Monitor web application logs for unusual requests, error codes, and exploit signatures. Use WAF rules.

name: Valid Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining initial access, persistence, privilege escalation, or defense evasion.
detection: Monitor for anomalous account usage such as logins from unusual locations, times, or devices.

name: External Remote Services
description: Adversaries may leverage external-facing remote services to initially access or persist within a network, such as VPNs, Citrix, and RDP.
detection: Monitor for unusual remote access connections, especially from unexpected geographic locations or at unusual times.

name: Supply Chain Compromise
description: Adversaries may manipulate products or product delivery mechanisms prior to receipt by a final consumer for the purpose of data or system compromise.
detection: Verify software integrity via hashes and code signing. Monitor for unexpected software updates or changes.

name: Command and Scripting Interpreter
description: Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.
detection: Monitor for suspicious command-line activity, script execution, and unusual interpreter usage.

name: PowerShell
description: Adversaries may abuse PowerShell commands and scripts for execution. PowerShell is a powerful interactive command-line interface and scripting environment included in Windows.
detection: Enable PowerShell script block logging and module logging. Monitor for encoded commands and suspicious cmdlets.

name: Windows Command Shell
description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows command shell (cmd.exe) for execution of commands and scripts.
detection: Monitor for suspicious cmd.exe child processes and unusual command-line arguments.

name: User Execution
description: Adversaries may rely upon specific actions by a user in order to gain execution, such as opening a malicious file or clicking a link.
detection: Monitor for suspicious file opens, especially from email clients or web browsers, and subsequent process creation.

name: Scheduled Task/Job
description: Adversaries may abuse task scheduling functionality to facilitate initial or recurring execution of malicious code.
detection: Monitor for creation of new scheduled tasks or cron jobs, especially those running as elevated users.

name: Exploitation for Client Execution
description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities in client applications to execute code, such as browser or office application exploits.
detection: Monitor for unusual child processes of common applications like browsers and office suites.

name: Account Manipulation
description: Adversaries may manipulate accounts to maintain access to victim systems, including modifying credentials or permission groups.
detection: Monitor for unexpected changes to user accounts, group memberships, and account properties.

name: Create Account
description: Adversaries may create accounts to maintain access to victim systems. Accounts may be created on the local system, domain, or cloud tenant.
detection: Monitor for new account creation events, especially accounts with elevated privileges or created outside normal processes.

name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
description: Adversaries may configure system settings to automatically execute a program during system boot or logon to maintain persistence.
detection: Monitor registry run keys, startup folders, and init scripts for unexpected modifications.

name: Server Software Component
description: Adversaries may abuse legitimate extensible development features of servers to establish persistent access, such as web shells or SQL triggers.
detection: Monitor web server directories for unexpected file additions. Use file integrity monitoring on web roots.

name: Web Shell
description: Adversaries may backdoor web servers with web shells to establish persistent access to systems.
detection: Monitor web server directories for new or modified files. Analyze HTTP traffic for web shell patterns.

name: Create or Modify System Process
description: Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.
detection: Monitor for new services, daemons, or systemd units. Track changes to existing system processes.

name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
description: Adversaries may exploit software vulnerabilities to elevate privileges. Exploitation of a software vulnerability occurs when an adversary takes advantage of a programming error in a program, service, or within the operating system itself.
detection: Monitor for unusual process behavior, unexpected privilege changes, and exploitation indicators.

name: Abuse Elevation Control Mechanism
description: Adversaries may circumvent mechanisms designed to control elevate privileges to gain higher-level permissions, such as UAC bypass or sudo exploitation.
detection: Monitor for UAC bypass attempts, sudo misconfigurations, and SetUID/SetGID abuse.

name: Access Token Manipulation
description: Adversaries may modify access tokens to operate under a different user or system security context to perform actions and bypass access controls.
detection: Monitor for token manipulation APIs (e.g., AdjustTokenPrivileges) and unexpected token impersonation.

name: Domain Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of domain accounts for privilege escalation within a domain environment.
detection: Monitor for anomalous domain account activity, including unusual logon patterns and privilege changes.

name: Indicator Removal
description: Adversaries may delete or alter generated artifacts on a host system, including logs, captured data, and other evidence of activity.
detection: Monitor for log clearing events, timestamp modifications, and file deletions in sensitive directories.

name: Clear Windows Event Logs
description: Adversaries may clear Windows Event Logs to hide the activity of an intrusion.
detection: Monitor for Event Log clearing events (Event ID 1102). Forward logs to a centralized SIEM.

name: Masquerading
description: Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and security tools.
detection: Monitor for files or processes with names similar to legitimate system files but in unusual locations.

name: Impair Defenses
description: Adversaries may maliciously modify components of a victim environment in order to hinder or disable defensive mechanisms.
detection: Monitor for disabled security tools, modified firewall rules, or stopped security services.

name: Disable or Modify Tools
description: Adversaries may modify or disable security tools to avoid detection of their malware or activity.
detection: Monitor for attempts to stop or modify security services like antivirus or EDR agents.

name: Obfuscated Files or Information
description: Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents.
detection: Monitor for encoded or encrypted payloads, base64 strings in command lines, and packed executables.

name: System Binary Proxy Execution
description: Adversaries may bypass process or signature-based defenses by proxying execution of malicious content with signed or trusted binaries (living off the land).
detection: Monitor for unusual use of signed binaries like mshta.exe, rundll32.exe, or regsvr32.exe.

name: OS Credential Dumping
description: Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.
detection: Monitor for access to LSASS process, SAM database, and credential store files. Watch for tools like Mimikatz.

name: LSASS Memory
description: Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS).
detection: Monitor for processes accessing LSASS memory. Enable Credential Guard and LSASS protection.

name: Brute Force
description: Adversaries may use brute force techniques to attempt access to accounts when passwords are unknown or when password hashes are obtained.
detection: Monitor for multiple failed authentication attempts from single sources or against single accounts.

name: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
description: Adversaries may attempt to subvert Kerberos authentication by stealing or forging Kerberos tickets to enable lateral movement or privilege escalation.
detection: Monitor for unusual Kerberos ticket requests, especially TGS requests for service accounts (Kerberoasting).

name: Credentials from Password Stores
description: Adversaries may search for common password storage locations to obtain user credentials, including browser password managers and credential vaults.
detection: Monitor for access to browser credential stores, password manager files, and credential vault APIs.

name: Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation
description: Adversaries may attempt to bypass MFA by generating repeated push notifications or challenges to wear down the user into accepting (MFA fatigue).
detection: Monitor for rapid successive MFA requests to a single user. Alert on MFA denials followed by approval.

name: Network Service Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts and local network infrastructure devices, including those that may be vulnerable to remote exploitation.
detection: Monitor for port scanning activity, especially from internal hosts to internal networks.

name: System Information Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get detailed information about the operating system and hardware, including version, patches, and architecture.
detection: Monitor for commands like systeminfo, uname, or hostnamectl executed in suspicious contexts.

name: File and Directory Discovery
description: Adversaries may enumerate files and directories or search in specific locations of a host or network share for certain information.
detection: Monitor for excessive directory listing commands or file enumeration activity from compromised accounts.

name: Permission Groups Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover group and permission settings to determine which user accounts and groups are available.
detection: Monitor for commands like net group, Get-ADGroup, or ldapsearch querying group memberships.

name: Remote System Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to discover other systems on the network by querying network services or using scanning tools.
detection: Monitor for ARP scans, ping sweeps, and net view commands from internal hosts.

name: Account Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of accounts on a system or within an environment, including local, domain, and cloud accounts.
detection: Monitor for account enumeration commands like net user, Get-ADUser, or cloud IAM queries.

name: Remote Services
description: Adversaries may use valid accounts to log into a service specifically designed to accept remote connections, such as RDP, SSH, or SMB.
detection: Monitor for unusual remote service connections between internal systems, especially during off-hours.

name: Remote Desktop Protocol
description: Adversaries may use Remote Desktop Protocol (RDP) to move laterally within a network by connecting to remote systems.
detection: Monitor for unusual RDP connections, especially between workstations or from unexpected source systems.

name: SMB/Windows Admin Shares
description: Adversaries may use Windows administrative shares (C$, ADMIN$, IPC$) for lateral movement and file copying between systems.
detection: Monitor for access to administrative shares from unexpected sources and SMB traffic between workstations.

name: Lateral Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files between systems in a compromised environment using internal network protocols.
detection: Monitor for file transfers between internal systems using SMB, SCP, or other protocols outside normal patterns.

name: Use Alternate Authentication Material
description: Adversaries may use alternate authentication material, such as password hashes, Kerberos tickets, or tokens, to move laterally within an environment.
detection: Monitor for pass-the-hash, pass-the-ticket, and token impersonation attacks in authentication logs.

name: Data from Local System
description: Adversaries may search local system sources, such as file systems and databases, to find files of interest and sensitive data prior to exfiltration.
detection: Monitor for unusual file access patterns, especially to sensitive directories and document repositories.

name: Data Staged
description: Adversaries may stage collected data in a central location or directory prior to exfiltration to minimize the number of connections made.
detection: Monitor for creation of archives or large files in temporary directories or unusual staging locations.

name: Archive Collected Data
description: Adversaries may compress and/or encrypt data that is collected prior to exfiltration using utilities like zip, 7zip, or tar.
detection: Monitor for execution of archiving tools and creation of compressed files, especially in unusual locations.

name: Automated Collection
description: Adversaries may use automated techniques for collecting data, such as scripts that search for and copy specific file types.
detection: Monitor for scripts or processes systematically accessing or copying files across directories.

name: Email Collection
description: Adversaries may target user email to collect sensitive information, including email forwarding rules and mailbox access.
detection: Monitor for new email forwarding rules, mailbox delegation changes, and unusual email access patterns.

name: Application Layer Protocol
description: Adversaries may communicate using OSI application layer protocols to avoid detection by blending in with existing traffic (HTTP, HTTPS, DNS).
detection: Monitor for unusual HTTP/HTTPS traffic patterns, DNS tunneling indicators, and beaconing behavior.

name: Web Protocols
description: Adversaries may communicate using HTTP/HTTPS to avoid detection by blending C2 traffic with normal web browsing.
detection: Monitor for beaconing patterns in HTTP/HTTPS traffic and connections to suspicious domains.

name: Ingress Tool Transfer
description: Adversaries may transfer tools or other files from an external system into a compromised environment using the command and control channel.
detection: Monitor for file downloads from external sources, especially executables and scripts from unusual domains.

name: Protocol Tunneling
description: Adversaries may tunnel network communications to and from a victim system within a separate protocol to avoid detection and network filtering.
detection: Monitor for DNS tunneling, ICMP tunneling, and other protocol encapsulation techniques.

name: Proxy
description: Adversaries may use a connection proxy to direct network traffic between systems or act as an intermediary for network communications to avoid direct connections to C2 infrastructure.
detection: Monitor for unusual proxy configurations, SOCKS proxy usage, and multi-hop connections.

name: Encrypted Channel
description: Adversaries may employ encryption to conceal command and control traffic, using standard or custom encryption protocols.
detection: Monitor for encrypted traffic to unusual destinations, self-signed certificates, and non-standard TLS implementations.

name: Exfiltration Over C2 Channel
description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an existing command and control channel, encoding data within normal C2 communications.
detection: Monitor for unusually large outbound data transfers over C2 channels and data encoding in network traffic.

name: Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol
description: Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over a different protocol than the existing command and control channel, such as DNS, FTP, or SMTP.
detection: Monitor for unusual outbound traffic on protocols not typically used for data transfer, such as DNS or ICMP.

name: Exfiltration Over Web Service
description: Adversaries may use legitimate web services such as cloud storage, pastebins, or code repositories to exfiltrate data.
detection: Monitor for large uploads to cloud storage services, pastebins, and other web services outside normal business patterns.

name: Exfiltration Over Unencrypted Non-C2 Protocol
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data over an unencrypted non-C2 network protocol such as FTP, HTTP, or DNS without encryption.
detection: Monitor for large outbound data transfers over cleartext protocols to external destinations.

name: Data Encrypted for Impact
description: Adversaries may encrypt data on target systems to interrupt availability. This is commonly associated with ransomware attacks.
detection: Monitor for mass file encryption events, unusual file extension changes, and high file I/O activity.

name: Service Stop
description: Adversaries may stop or disable services on a system to render those services unavailable to legitimate users, often as part of ransomware operations.
detection: Monitor for unexpected stopping of critical services, especially security tools and database services.

name: Inhibit System Recovery
description: Adversaries may delete or remove built-in operating system data and turn off services designed to aid in the recovery of a corrupted system, such as shadow copies and backups.
detection: Monitor for deletion of shadow copies (vssadmin delete shadows), backup catalog modifications, and recovery partition changes.

name: System Shutdown/Reboot
description: Adversaries may shutdown or reboot systems to interrupt access to, or aid in the destruction of, those systems.
detection: Monitor for unexpected system shutdown or reboot commands, especially outside maintenance windows.

name: Data Destruction
description: Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems, services, and network resources.
detection: Monitor for mass file deletion events, disk wiping tools, and unusual file modification patterns.

name: Network Denial of Service
description: Adversaries may perform network denial of service (DoS) attacks to degrade or block the availability of targeted resources to users.
detection: Monitor for unusual spikes in network traffic volume, SYN floods, and volumetric attack patterns.

name: Windows Management Instrumentation
description: Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads.
detection: Monitor for WMI process creation events and unusual WMI query patterns.

name: Scheduled Task
description: Adversaries may abuse the Windows Task Scheduler to perform task scheduling for initial or recurring execution of malicious code.
detection: Monitor for creation of new scheduled tasks via schtasks.exe or Task Scheduler COM interfaces.

name: Process Injection
description: Adversaries may inject code into processes to evade process-based defenses as well as possibly elevate privileges.
detection: Monitor for suspicious cross-process access, unusual memory allocations, and DLL injection indicators.

name: Deobfuscate/Decode Files or Information
description: Adversaries may use obfuscated files or information to hide artifacts of an intrusion from analysis, using utilities to deobfuscate at runtime.
detection: Monitor for execution of decoding utilities like certutil, base64, or custom deobfuscation scripts.

name: Modify Authentication Process
description: Adversaries may modify authentication mechanisms and processes to access user credentials or enable otherwise unwarranted access to accounts.
detection: Monitor for changes to authentication libraries, PAM modules, or SSO configurations.

name: SSH
description: Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to log into remote machines using Secure Shell (SSH) for lateral movement.
detection: Monitor for unusual SSH connections between internal systems, key-based authentication from unexpected sources.

name: System Owner/User Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to identify the primary user, currently logged in user, or set of users that commonly use a system.
detection: Monitor for commands like whoami, query user, and w that enumerate user information.

name: Process Discovery
description: Adversaries may attempt to get information about running processes on a system to understand installed software and security tools.
detection: Monitor for process listing commands like tasklist, ps, or Get-Process in suspicious contexts.

name: Trusted Relationship
description: Adversaries may breach or leverage organizations that have access to intended victims through trusted third-party relationships.
detection: Monitor for unusual access from third-party connections and VPN links. Audit third-party access regularly.

name: DNS
description: Adversaries may communicate using the Domain Name System (DNS) application layer protocol to avoid detection by blending in with DNS traffic.
detection: Monitor for unusual DNS query patterns, high volume of DNS requests, long domain names, and TXT record queries.

name: Data Encoding
description: Adversaries may encode data to make the content of command and control traffic more difficult to detect.
detection: Monitor for base64-encoded content in network traffic and unusual data patterns in HTTP headers or payloads.

name: Cloud Accounts
description: Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of cloud accounts to gain initial access to cloud environments.
detection: Monitor for cloud account logins from unusual locations, impossible travel scenarios, and privilege escalation.

name: Transfer Data to Cloud Account
description: Adversaries may exfiltrate data by transferring the data to another cloud account they control within the same cloud service provider.
detection: Monitor for data transfers to external cloud accounts or unusual cross-account access patterns.

name: Unix Shell
description: Adversaries may abuse Unix shell commands and scripts for execution on Linux and macOS systems.
detection: Monitor for suspicious shell command execution, reverse shells, and unusual script activity.

name: Adversary-in-the-Middle
description: Adversaries may position themselves between two or more networked devices to intercept and relay network communications (MITM attacks).
detection: Monitor for ARP spoofing, LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, and unexpected network redirections.

name: Virtualization/Sandbox Evasion
description: Adversaries may employ various means to detect and avoid virtualization and analysis environments to hinder forensic investigation.
detection: Monitor for processes checking for VM artifacts, sandbox detection routines, or timing-based evasion.
